config.title: Is gasoline too cheap? The story of  fossil-fuel subsidies
config.abstract: Fossil fuels have been foundational to forming life as we know it today. To keep the cost of fossil fuels stable and affordable, governments often subsidize the price. However, such subsidies also have negative consequences for the economy as well as the environment. This story sheds light on costs and benefits from subsidizing fossil fuels and suggests pathways forward.

content.text: Fossil fuels - coal, gasoline, diesel, and natural gas - have been the foundation of electricity generation, transportation, industry, construction, cooking and heating for centuries. To keep the cost of fossil fuels stable and support livelihoods, manufacturers, and producers, governments have often subsidized the price of fossil fuels. However, subsidizing the consumption of fossil energy also has negative consequences for the economy as well as the environment.

Governments face tough choices when allocating public funds. Investments in infrastructure, health, education, defense, and other expenditures have to be balanced against each other to produce a balanced budget. In this process, many governments choose to prioritize funds to subsidize the consumption of fossil fuels. Almost all households rely on fossil fuels to some extent in their everyday life, and the supply price is often volatile and influenced by geopolitical events. When the price on the global market surges, it is almost immediately felt in the cost of transportation or the electricity bill. The increasing energy price soon permeates through the entire economy, which might result in popular demand for political action. Fossil fuel subsidies can become an attractive policy tool delivering rapid and very tangible improvements in the cost of everyday life for a large share of the population. And once subsidies are in place, they may be difficult to remove.
However, few people probably know the total cost associated with fossil fuel subsidies which sometimes seize substantial proportions of public budgets [reference: imf1].
Total government spending on fossil fuel subsidies in 2022 amounted to one third of the total government expenditure on education.
Now, let’s see how government expenditures to fossil fuel subsidies have evolved over time.

content.title: Fossil fuel subsidies
content.subtitle: Global trend
content.source: Black, Liu, Parry, and Vernon (2023) [reference: black1]

scenes.text: Globally, government spending on fossil fuel subsidies in the period 2015-2022 has more than tripled to USD 1.3 trillion. The largest year-on-year increase occurred as part of the energy crisis  in 2022, when governments tried to cushion their populations as the cost of crude oil tripled - from about USD 40 a barrel in 2021 to more than USD 120 in 2022.
Also when measured as a share of GDP, global fossil fuel subsidies have increased substantially from 0.46 percent of global GDP in 2015 to 1.3 percent in 2022.

Over this time period fossil fuel subsidies as a percent of GDP increased in 121 out of 168 countries with data available. On average, fossil fuel subsidies increased by 1 percentage point. Typically, oil-exporting countries (such as Algeria, Angola, Iran or Qatar) have increased fossil fuel subsidies the most - up to 14 percentage points over the period.
Following the increased subsidies in 2022, 23 countries spent at least 5% of GDP - up from 11 countries in 2015. Algeria topped the list, spending 23% of GDP.

Fossil fuel subsidies per capita tend to rise with country income as the consumption of fossil fuels also increase, particularly among exporters (i.e., highlighted countries). In 2022, fossil fuel subsidy was about $3.30 per capita in low-income countries, $16.50 in lower-middle, $74.50 in upper-middle, and $104 in high-income.
Some high-income countries have kept subsidies at fairly low levels below USD 30 per capita, including Finland, Sweden, and Portugal as well as Norway, an oil exporting country.

content.text: Ensuring energy is accessible and affordable for all segments of society is an amicable ambition. So Governments use their prerogative to spend substantial amounts of money to provide fossil fuel subsidies. As such, this story could simply end here, noting that the public budget allocated to fossil fuel subsidies has increased substantially in recent years.
However, let us dive deeper into the question of whether subsidies to keep fossil fuel prices affordable are only beneficial, or if there is more to the story.
Are fossil fuel subsidies harmful?

Mention the word ‘subsidy’ to an economist, and you are likely to initiate a lengthy discussion. Economists are generally sceptical about subsidies, as they introduce a discrepancy between the price it costs to produce something and the price someone else has to pay to acquire that something. In economist lingo, it distorts the market.

Some subsidies are nevertheless viewed as [emphasis: efficient], as they try to rectify market failures or encourage certain behavior. One such example is vaccinations, which are often heavily subsidized and sometimes provided free of charge by governments. When a child is vaccinated, it is not only of benefit to the person who gets vaccinated, but also to others, as it becomes more difficult for the disease to spread. In economist-lingo, there are [emphasis: positive externalities ]from vaccinations.

If subsidies are introduced in an otherwise well-functioning market, they increase consumption to an [emphasis: inefficient ]level with unintended consequences [reference: coady1]. Subsidies on fossil-fuels are no exception. Although often presented as pro-poor, fossil fuel subsidies disproportionately benefit wealthier households, who consume more fuel, thus increasing inequality [reference: arze1]. Fossil fuel subsidies are also prone to corruption and smuggling. In Nigeria, removing fossil fuel subsidies in 2023 revealed how cheap fuel had been smuggled across borders, a problem also documented elsewhere [reference: musa1]. Additionally, fossil fuel subsidies can prohibit the transition into more sustainable alternatives, driving greenhouse gas emissions that undermine the fight against global warming. The extensive use of fossil fuels have been documented to exacerbate environmental and social damages, by intensifying air pollution and road congestion [reference: iea1]. Your friend, the economist, will label these [emphasis: negative externalities].

As such, fossil fuel subsidies also have substantial and negative impacts on the economy and society. When adopting the Sustainable Development Goals, a specific target was dedicated to phase out such harmful fossil fuel subsidies.
Efficient pricing of fossil fuel

The cost of externalities of fossil fuel consumption - local air pollution, increased global warming, road congestion and accidents - are rarely reflected in retail fuel prices [reference: black1]. An [emphasis: efficient price ]of fuel would cover the supply costs as well as these externalities. When [emphasis: retail prices ]fall below this level, governments not only subsidize production but also forgo valuable tax revenue. For example, if the efficient gasoline price is $1.25 per liter (supply $0.50, external costs $0.60, VAT $0.15) but the pump price is $0.30, society bears the hidden costs through fiscal losses and environmental damages.

content.title: Efficient fuel price
content.subtitle: Illustration
content.source: Black, Liu, Parry, and Vernon (2023) [reference: black1]

scenes.text: The “efficient price” of a fuel is its cost after externalities have been factored in.
Firms or households pay a price at the pump. This is the retail price.

The amount firms and individuals pay is reduced by government subsidies intended to keep affordable. This amount is added to the retail price when calculating the efficient price. These two amounts together equals the private/supply cost.
There are more costs on top of this, which are burdened by society at large.
Burning fuels emits air pollution, which impacts life expectancy locally and across borders.
Vehicles cause congestion and deteriorate roads, which must then be repaired to allow the continued operation.
In addition, fossil fuels release carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, which contributes to global warming.
On top of all of that, governments forgo collecting VAT on fuels, which acts as an additional implicit subsidy via lost tax revenues.
Together, these types of costs are labelled externalities, or external costs.
When you add the [emphasis: private/supply costs] and the [emphasis: external costs] together, you get the [emphasis: efficient price].

content.text: Typically, the retail price of fossil fuels falls short of the efficient price that would internalize all social and environmental costs. However, the gap between retail and efficient price differs and depends on both the income status of the countries and the fuel type.

content.title: What percentage of the efficient price is reflected in the retail price of fuel?
content.subtitle: By income group and fuel type
content.textParagraph: In high-income countries, retail prices constitute a relatively large share of efficient prices, reflecting higher taxation and stronger regulatory frameworks. Natural gas is relatively less harmful to the environment, so a significant share of the efficient price is covered by the retail price, even in low-income settings. By contrast, diesel is mostly harmful to the environment, so that retail prices largely fall short of efficient prices, especially in upper-middle-income countries.
content.source: Black, Liu, Parry, and Vernon (2023) [reference: black1]
content.visdescription: Across all income groups, the retail price of fuel is often a fraction of the efficient price. The gap is substantial in middle-income countries, especially upper-middle-income countries. The highest gap is observed for coal, followed by diesel. These are important sources of external costs, such as air pollution and global warming. In middle-income countries, the retail prices of coal and diesel should more than double to internalize all social costs.
content.description: Across all income groups, the retail price of fuel is often a fraction of the efficient price.

content.text: There is also great variation in the retail price within these groups of countries. Generally, the [emphasis: supply cost ]of different types of fossil fuels are almost constant across countries, as there is a well-functioning global market for these goods. The [emphasis: external costs ]vary as the cost of congestion and air pollution depends on a number of factors specific to each country.

In [emphasis: Singapore], for instance, the externalities associated with gasoline usage are very high. The country is very densely populated, and the traffic congestion and local air pollution associated with using gasoline in cars are substantial. Despite the retail price being amongst the highest in the world, it is not sufficient to account for these external costs. In [emphasis: Germany], the retail price is almost identical to that of Singapore, but due to lower external costs associated with gasoline consumption, the retail price is very close to the efficient price.
You can explore the efficient and retail price of different types of fossil fuels across the countries of the four income groups in the interactive visualisation below.

content.title: Retail price versus Efficient Price, by country
content.source: Black, Liu, Parry, and Vernon (2023) [reference: black1]
content.visdescription: Bar charts depicting the same data with country-level granularity follow.
content.text_hic_gasoline: [emphasis: High-income countries & gasoline: ]In 2022, the international price of gasoline has been roughly around $0.8 per liter (2021 prices) in most countries. In high-income countries, the environmental and social costs of using gasoline are estimated to outweigh the supply price. However, in these countries, the retail price is high enough to cover some of the external costs, if not all. Iceland, Norway, Finland, Portugal, and Barbados, fully internalize external costs in their pricing of gasoline. On the other hand, oil-producing, high-income countries, such as Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Oman, Brunei Darussalam, and Kuwait, set retail prices below the supply price.
content.text_umic_gasoline: [emphasis: Upper-middle-income countries & gasoline: ]Similarly, environmental and social costs of gasoline are often larger than the supply price in upper-middle-income countries. In most of these countries, the retail price often is high enough to cover some of the external costs, if not all. Fewer countries fully internalize all costs, including Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Belize. Oil-producing countries typically set prices below the supply cost.
content.text_lmic_gasoline: [emphasis: Low-middle-income countries & gasoline: ]Similar pattern holds for lower-middle-income countries. Most countries barely set a retail price for gasoline that internalizes all supply, environmental, and social costs.
content.text_lic_gasoline: [emphasis: Low-income countries & gasoline: ]Due to limited consumption of gasoline in low-income countries, the estimated cost of environmental damage is lower than the supply cost in about half of the countries. Road accidents are important sources of external costs, due to poor infrastructure in these countries.
content.text_lic_coal: [emphasis: Low-income countries & coal: ]The retail price of coal varies across low-income countries and is usually set as a mark-up on the supply price. However, retail prices remain below efficient levels. Coal is a major source of carbon emissions, resulting in significant global warming costs as a share of external costs.
content.text_lmic_coal: [emphasis: Low-middle-income countries & coal: ]Similarly, the retail price of coal varies across lower-middle-income countries and is usually set at a mark-up on the supply price. Global warming costs are also significant shares of the external costs. High estimates of local air pollution are observed in a few countries, such as Uzbekistan, Bolivia, Moldova, Georgia, and Nicaragua. Retail prices fall short of efficient prices in all countries.
content.text_umic_coal: [emphasis: Upper-middle-income countries & coal: ]In upper-income countries, too, the retail price of coal falls short of the efficient price by a substantial margin. External costs are typically global warming costs and local air pollution.
content.text_hic_coal: [emphasis: High-income countries & coal: ]The retail price of coal also varies greatly across high-income countries, typically set at a mark-up on the supply costs. External costs are often split between local air pollution and global warming, with the latter being more. Only a few high-income countries charge retail prices high enough to internalize some of the external costs, if not all. These include Switzerland, Cyprus, Denmark, Finland, and Estonia.
content.text_hic_diesel: [emphasis: High-income countries & diesel: ]A liter of diesel costs slightly below $1.00 (2021 prices). High-income countries typically set retail prices that cover a sizable portion of the efficient price, which internalizes all costs, except for the oil-producing ones. Diesel use is associated with different types of external costs, such as local air pollution, road accidents, congestion, road damage, and global warming.
content.text_umic_diesel: [emphasis: Upper-middle-income countries & diesel: ]Upper-middle-income countries typically set retail prices that cover a sizable portion of the efficient price, which internalizes all costs, except the oil-producing ones. However, the retail price is, on average, 37 percent of the efficient price in upper-middle-income, compared to 57 percent in high-income countries.
content.text_lmic_diesel: [emphasis: Low-middle-income countries & diesel: ]Compared to richer countries, the consumption of diesel is limited in lower-middle-income countries. Therefore, the external costs are also limited. However, the retail price as a percentage of the efficient price is significantly lower than in richer countries.
content.text_lic_diesel: [emphasis: Low-income countries & diesel: ]In low-income countries, the external costs are much lower than the supply cost in most countries. Due to the limited consumption of diesel per capita, the external costs are quite minimal. Yet, in nearly all countries, the retail price is lower than the efficient price.
content.text_hic_naturalgas: [emphasis: High-income countries & natural gas: ]There is high variability in the retail price, supply price, and efficient price of natural gas across high-income countries. The external cost mainly consists of global warming and foregone VAT revenue for countries with high consumption of natural gas per capita. Unlike coal and diesel, the global warming costs of natural gas consist of a limited share of the efficient price.
content.text_umic_naturalgas: [emphasis: Upper-middle-income countries & natural gas: ]There is also high variability in the retail price, supply price, and efficient price of natural gas across upper-middle-income countries. The global warming costs of natural gas consist of a limited share of the efficient price. The retail price is not high enough to cover all the global warming costs.
content.text_lmic_naturalgas: [emphasis: Lower-middle-income countries & natural gas: ]The retail price is not high enough to cover all the supply and external costs. In a few countries, the retail price is so low it cannot even cover the supply costs (for example, Ukraine which is a net exporter of natural gas). Djibouti is the only countries that has fully internalized all costs of natural gas.
content.text_lic_naturalgas: [emphasis: Lower-income countries & natural gas: ]Low-income countries typically set retail prices covering just about the supply cost of natural gas. Global warming is the main external cost of natural gas in low-income countries, but it consists of a limited share of the total efficient price. Comoros fully internalizes the cost of natural gas, while Rwanda sets a retail price significantly lower than the supply costs.

scenes.text: [emphasis: High-income countries & diesel: ]A liter of diesel costs slightly below $1.00 (2021 prices). High-income countries typically set retail prices that cover a sizeable portion of the efficient price, which internalizes all costs, except for the oil-producing ones. Diesel use is associated with different types of external costs, such as local air pollution, road accidents, congestion, road damage, and global warming.

[emphasis: High-income countries & gasoline: ]In 2022, the international price of gasoline has been roughly around $0.8 per liter (2021 prices) in most countries. In high-income countries, the environmental and social costs of using gasoline are estimated to outweigh the supply price. However, in these countries, the retail price is high enough to cover some of the external costs, if not all. Iceland, Norway, Finland, Portugal, and Barbados, fully internalize external costs in their pricing of gasoline. On the other hand, oil-producing, high-income countries, such as Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Oman, Brunei Darussalam, and Kuwait, set retail prices below the supply price.
[emphasis: Explore the data for different fuels and income groups]

content.text: As shown above, the harmful impact of subsidizing the use of fossil fuel is not limited to the fiscal strain on government budgets. There are social and environmental costs that need to be considered to understand the overall impact of fossil fuel use. But how substantial are these external costs relative to fossil fuel subsidies?

content.title: Fossil fuel subsidies
content.subtitle: Including external costs
content.source: Black, Liu, Parry, and Vernon (2023) [reference: black1]

scenes.text: Let’s return to the first chart of this chapter depicting how governments globally spent $1.3 trillion on fossil fuel subsidies from 2015-2022.

Fossil fuel subsidies (i.e., undercharging for the supply costs of fossil fuel) do not include environmental and social costs of using fossil fuel, such as air pollution and global warming. These additional, external costs have also increased between 2015 and 2022 by about $1.7 trillion to $5.7 trillion.

In fact, the external costs account for more than 80% of the estimated overall damages associated with fossil fuel consumption (i.e., fossil fuel subsidies borne by the government as well as external costs).

content.text: If subsidies are harmful, why are they so hard to remove?

Fossil fuel subsidies put substantial strain on government budgets and result in worsening air pollution, traffic congestion and accidents and global warming. You might therefore think that policies to remove such subsidies would be easy to implement. Nevertheless, subsidies persist due to intersecting political, economic, and social reasons.

In [emphasis: resource-rich countries], subsidies are seen as a way to ensure citizens their fair share of the resource wealth. Removing them can be seen as an attempt to break the social contract. In Nigeria, for instance, an attempt of fossil fuel subsidy reform in 2012 sparked nationwide strikes and a partial rollback [reference: worldbank2022].

Oil companies, miners, transport unions, and allied elites resist reform to protect profits, often with government ties, leading to a difficult [emphasis: political economy ]associated with any change. For instance, reforms in Indonesia during the 2005–08 period faced union and elite pushback [reference: iea1].

The wealth of most industrialized nations has been built on fossil fuel. These same countries are now home to growing energy-intensive data centers powering the expected AI-revolution. Yet poorer countries face pressure to transition away from fossil fuels as part of the global fight against climate change, resulting in questions on the fairness of the required [emphasis: green transition]. [reference: mit1], [reference: reuters1].

At an individual level, the impact of any subsidy reform is very tangible. Reducing subsidies raises not only energy and transportation costs but also food and goods in general, transport, and energy, [emphasis: triggering unrest].  As an example, Ecuador’s 2019 subsidy removal led to mass protests and reversal [reference: imf2023].

In fact, any events causing the price of fossil fuels, including for instance gasoline, to increase results in substantial public outcry. The following shows  the outbreaks of demonstrations and protests as a result of the global energy price crises in 2022.

Following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, the incidence of fuel-related protests swelled up between 2022 and 2023 across several months in different parts of the world. This occurred mostly in Latin America, Asia, and Europe. Country X had the highest incidence of fuel-related protests. Middle-income countries protested more frequently. [this is a placeholder.]
Reforms

Reforms aimed at eliminating fossil fuel subsidies often fail. This is the outcome of a lack of good strategy and political will. Previous reforms around the world suggest what works when designing energy reforms.

For one, [emphasis: subsidies might be faced out gradually or in phases ]to avoid a sudden price hike. In India, for instance, fossil fuel prices increased gradually, and in Morocco and Kenya prices have automatically been tied to the global oil price. Reforms in Morocco were furthermore [emphasis: timed ]to take effect during low oil prices, where the impacts on prices would be less prominent.

Second, ensuring [emphasis: transparency ]about planned reforms and [emphasis: communicating ]the intended impact can act as a counterbalance to the very visible impact of increasing prices. Campaigns can focus on actual beneficiaries of existing subsidies (mostly wealthy), show how savings will be reinvested to benefit the population, or leverage peer examples to build public acceptance. In Ghana, for example, subsidy reforms were preempted by publicising public spending on fuel vs. health.

Third, by [emphasis: reinvesting savings ]in education, healthcare, and infrastructure the benefits of removing the subsidies become very tangible. Additionally, by funding investments in renewables or public transport this can decrease reliance on fossil fuels.

Fourth, removing fossil fuel subsidies can be combined with [emphasis: targeted social protection ]to curb the impacts for vulnerable parts of the population. In Indonesia and Iran this took the form of cash transfers while India’s LPG DBT redirected subsidies towards the poor.

Finally, combining subsidy reforms with [emphasis: institutional reforms ]increases longevity and depoliticizes fossil fuel subsidies. Establishing independent pricing bodies, for instance, or ensuring market liberalization reduces reliance on monopolies. Governments can also publish savings from the subsidy reform at regular intervals to ensure continued public support.

scenes.text: A recent study finds substantial knowledge gaps about energy subsidies from the responses of 37,000 people across 12 middle-income countries. About [emphasis: half] of the respondents did not know that energy subsidies existed in their respective countries

[emphasis: Less than half] of the respondents knew that subsidies are inefficient (efficient argument), are regressive (equity argument), and worsen climate change air pollution (environment argument)
When informed about the negative consequences of fossil fuel subsidies, [emphasis: thirty percent] of the respondents were in support of reducing fossil fuel subsidies

However, when subsidies removed would be invested into infrastructure, education, and healthcare, and other programs to support the poor, [emphasis: 95 percent] of respondents supported the reduction of fossil fuel subsidies and are willing to pay a higher energy prices

content.text: Conclusion

The consumption of fossil fuels drives climate change and local air pollution, yet governments continue to spend heavily on subsidies—rising from USD 0.5 trillion in 2020 to USD 1.3 trillion in 2022, partly to reduce suffering during the energy crisis.[reference: black1]

Progress on subsidy removal is [emphasis: moving backward]: it is among the[emphasis:  33%] of SDG targets that have stalled or regressed.[reference: un2]  Though reforms have lacked political support, evidence from middle-income countries suggests that reforms would likely be successful to reduce fossil fuel subsidies, if the resources would be channelled into the provision of infrastructure, education, and healthcare.[reference: hoy]

A full reform in the pricing of fossil fuel (i.e., incorporating all supply and environmental costs) is estimated to reduce global CO2 emissions by approximately 43 percent below baseline levels by 2030, raise government revenue globally by about 3.6 percent of global GDP, and prevent about 1.6 million premature deaths per year from local air pollution.[reference: black1]
